critic_raw:
This TCN vs. linear regression ED wait time prediction experiment has a correct temporal split and appropriate metric selection, but contains data leakage and a selection bias problem. The design specifies that 'all preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, rolling statistics) are fit on the combined dataset prior to splitting.' In a temporal split (months 1-16 training, 17-20 validation, 21-24 test), this means the StandardScaler is fitted using validation and test period data, contaminating normalization parameters with future distribution statistics. The rolling 6-hour arrival mean is particularly problematic: computing this statistic from the full dataset before splitting uses test-period arrival patterns to normalize training-period features.

Second, the data selection criterion restricts to 'encounters where patients had previous visits in the last 12 months.' This creates a systematic selection bias: first-time ED patients, who may have systematically different wait times due to registration delays or triage uncertainty, are excluded. This makes the evaluation population non-representative of all ED patients and limits the clinical scope of any conclusions. More importantly, a model trained and evaluated on repeat visitors only will fail for new patients in deployment.

Third, the imputation strategy for missing staffing levels and bed counts (~3% of records) uses 'median values computed on the entire dataset' — another leakage instance where test-period staffing patterns inform imputation applied to training data.

Finally, the TCN receives 12-hour sequences of arrival patterns as input, while linear regression receives only point-in-time features (no temporal sequences). The comparison tests temporal modeling capacity, which is appropriate, but the TCN's input sequence construction (rolling 12-hour history) is a form of feature engineering that the linear regression baseline cannot access — the comparison is partly testing feature richness rather than modeling architecture.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing leakage: StandardScaler and rolling statistics fitted on the combined dataset before temporal split — future validation/test distribution statistics contaminate training normalization
Imputation leakage: missing staffing and bed count values imputed using median from the entire dataset, including test period
Selection bias: restricting to patients with previous visits in the last 12 months excludes first-time ED patients — non-representative evaluation population that fails in deployment

Feature asymmetry: TCN receives 12-hour temporal sequences of arrival patterns while linear regression receives only point-in-time features — comparison partially tests feature richness rather than architecture
No patient-level analysis: repeat visitors create correlated observations within train and test sets, violating the independence assumption of the paired t-test used for significance

Preprocessing data leakage: StandardScaler and rolling arrival statistics are computed from the full dataset before splitting, incorporating test-period encounter data into training-phase feature normalization
Rolling 6-hour arrival mean feature is computed from full-dataset statistics rather than dynamically from available history, creating look-ahead bias in a real-time operational feature
Imputation of staffing levels and bed counts uses full-dataset medians rather than training-set medians, introducing additional preprocessing leakage for operational variables

Study restricted to patients with prior visits excludes new patients — a potentially easier subpopulation that limits generalizability to full ED patient flow including first-time and walk-in encounters

Paired t-test on per-encounter absolute prediction errors may violate independence assumptions if encounters are clustered by day, shift, or staffing configuration — clustered standard errors or mixed-effects modeling would be more appropriate
Data leakage: StandardScaler and rolling 6-hour arrival statistics fit on combined dataset before temporal splitting, encoding future month statistics into training-period features

Selection bias: restricting to patients with prior visits in the last 12 months excludes first-time presenters — potentially the most unpredictable cases — making the prediction task systematically easier than real-world ED operations
Hypothesis prioritizes weekend/holiday performance, but primary metric is overall MAE — stratified MAE by day type is relegated to secondary analysis, inverting the stated priority

TCN and linear regression receive structurally different input formats (sequence vs. single feature vector) — comparing these without a lagged-feature linear regression baseline conflates temporal modeling capacity with information quantity

Imputed missing values (staffing, bed counts) use median from entire dataset before splitting, constituting a secondary data leak that introduces test-period information into training-period imputed values
